title: Chapter 3 — Using Liferafts: Detailed Study Guide
reconciliationNote: Interim source-backed guide built from the repository's copy of 46 CFR Subpart 160.151 — Inflatable Liferafts and the 23-item liferaft-parts worksheet with answer key (2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg). The actual Chapter 3 course-manual pages 35-88 remain missing. Accordingly, this guide does not label any new claim as course-manual-verified and explicitly preserves source limitations.

sections.title: 1. Source Boundary and How to Use This Guide
sections.whyThisMatters: Chapter 3 has enough official and visual material for serious study, but not enough to pretend the missing course manual has been recovered.
mustRemember: The actual Chapter 3 manual pages are not in the repository.
Federal regulation content is source-backed study material, not proof of the course manual's exact wording or emphasis.
Worksheet-number questions are visual-study material tied to one specific 23-item diagram.
sections.explanation: Use this guide to learn liferaft equipment, parts, deployment systems, boarding features, stability provisions, and maintenance expectations. Continue treating unsupported course-manual details cautiously.
confusionTraps: Do not transfer lifeboat rules into liferaft questions merely because the equipment names sound similar.
Do not treat a federal construction requirement as evidence that the course manual tests the fact in the same wording.

sourceReferences.note: Documents the missing manual-page gap and available Tier 2/3 sources.

sections.title: 2. Liferaft Parts and Diagram Identification
sections.whyThisMatters: Diagram questions test whether similar lines, valves, inflation parts, and boarding aids can be distinguished under pressure.
mustRemember: #2 Equipment bags
#4 Pressure relief valve
#5 Heaving line
#8 Rainwater catchment tube assembly
#9 Righting strap
#10 CO2 inflation system
#11 Deflation plug
#14 Buoyancy tube
#16 Operating cord / sea painter
#21 Boarding ladder
sections.explanation: Study the number-to-name and name-to-number direction. The hardest errors usually involve confusing the heaving line with the painter, the pressure relief valve with the deflation plug, or the righting strap with the boarding ladder.
confusionTraps: Heaving line (#5) is not the inflation-triggering sea painter (#16).
Pressure relief valve (#4) and deflation plug (#11) are separate components.
Righting strap (#9) is not the boarding ladder (#21).

sections.title: 3. Painter System, Inflation Trigger, and Container Release
sections.whyThisMatters: A deployment sequence can fail if the raft is thrown overboard but the controlling painter is not pulled to initiate inflation.
mustRemember: The painter must resist sunlight and salt-spray deterioration and water absorption.
Pulling the painter starts inflation through the linked painter-and-cylinder system.
The maximum identified pull force is 150 N, approximately 34 lb.
The raft must be able to break free of its container when inflation begins.
The container includes a tamper-evident telltale seal-and-wire.
sections.explanation: Think of the painter as both a control connection and the inflation trigger in the cited system. The container protects the packed raft but cannot remain locked around it during inflation.
processSteps: Deploy the packed liferaft container as directed.
Maintain control of the operating cord / sea painter.
Pull the painter to initiate inflation.
Confirm the raft breaks free of the container as inflation proceeds.
Manage the inflated raft using the appropriate lines and boarding features.
confusionTraps: Throwing the container overboard is not the same as pulling the painter.
The heaving line is not the inflation trigger.
A broken telltale seal shows possible disturbance; it does not certify serviceability.

sourceReferences.note: 46 CFR §160.151-17 painter, inflation, and container provisions.

sections.title: 4. Required Liferaft Equipment
sections.whyThisMatters: The required equipment list covers immediate survival, signaling, first aid, food and water, raft handling, and instructions. It is broad enough that plausible distractors are common.
mustRemember: Heaving line, jackknife, bailer, sponge, sea anchor, and paddles
Tin opener, first-aid kit, whistle, and electric torch
Rocket parachute flares, hand flares, buoyant smoke signals, radar reflector, and signaling mirror
Lifesaving-signals card and waterproof survival instructions
Fishing tackle, food rations, drinking water or approved desalting arrangement, and drinking cup
Anti-seasickness medicine and instructions for immediate action
sections.explanation: Learn the list by functional clusters rather than as 23 unrelated words: handling and water removal; signaling; medical and seasickness; food and water; written guidance; and basic tools.
confusionTraps: Useful shipboard equipment is not automatically required liferaft equipment.
The lifesaving-signals card and survival instructions are separate required written resources.
The drinking-water provision may involve drinking water or an approved desalting arrangement; do not substitute seawater.

sections.title: 5. Boarding, Righting, and Assisting Survivors
sections.whyThisMatters: A person in the water needs a stable, usable entry system; an overturned raft needs a different component and response.
mustRemember: Boarding-ladder steps must provide a suitable foothold.
The boarding ramp is rated for a 100 kg / 220 lb occupant.
The worksheet identifies the boarding ladder as #21.
The worksheet identifies the righting strap as #9.
A first-aid kit is part of the required equipment package.
sections.explanation: Separate three issues: restoring an overturned raft, helping someone enter an upright raft, and providing care after entry. The available sources support components and ratings but do not supply a complete course-manual casualty-transfer procedure.
confusionTraps: Do not use 'righting strap' and 'boarding ladder' interchangeably.
A boarding-ramp load rating does not by itself describe the complete method for moving an injured survivor.

sections.title: 6. Stability and Structural Components
sections.whyThisMatters: Inflatable rafts depend on several different systems to retain shape, manage pressure, and resist overturning.
mustRemember: Water-filled appendages deploy beneath the raft to improve stability.
The buoyancy tube is worksheet item #14.
The CO2 inflation system is worksheet item #10.
The pressure relief valve is worksheet item #4.
The deflation plug is worksheet item #11.
sections.explanation: The inflation system creates buoyancy, the buoyancy tube provides the primary inflated structure, pressure-management components protect or release air, and underside ballast appendages improve stability.
confusionTraps: Ballast appendages are not freshwater storage.
The pressure relief valve is not the same as a deliberate deflation plug.
The CO2 inflation system is not the painter itself; the painter triggers the linked inflation system.

sections.title: 7. Inspection, Servicing, and Records
sections.whyThisMatters: A packed raft can look untouched while still being overdue for inspection or servicing; readiness depends on both routine checks and formal servicing.
mustRemember: Perform a monthly external visual inspection using the checklist.
Periodic servicing is performed by an approved servicing facility.
Maintain a liferaft maintenance log.
The tamper-evident telltale seal-and-wire supports container-integrity inspection.
sections.explanation: Routine external inspection, approved-facility servicing, and maintenance records are separate parts of the readiness system. None replaces the others.
processSteps: Inspect the exterior and container condition monthly.
Check the telltale seal-and-wire for evidence of disturbance.
Document inspections and maintenance in the log.
Send the raft to an approved facility when periodic servicing is due.
confusionTraps: Monthly visual inspection is not a full inflation test.
A normal-looking container does not eliminate periodic servicing requirements.
Onboard inspection does not replace approved-facility servicing.

sections.title: 8. High-Value Exam Comparisons
sections.whyThisMatters: Most Chapter 3 errors come from swapping two plausible components or answering a neighboring stage of the deployment process.
mustRemember: Heaving line versus sea painter: the painter is linked to inflation.
Righting strap versus boarding ladder: overturn recovery versus entry.
Pressure relief valve versus deflation plug: automatic pressure management versus deliberate deflation access.
Monthly visual inspection versus periodic servicing: shipboard routine versus approved facility.
Required equipment versus structural part: what is carried aboard versus what is built into the raft.
sections.explanation: Before choosing an answer, identify whether the question is asking about a line, structural component, carried equipment item, deployment step, boarding aid, or maintenance layer.
confusionTraps: Answering with a generally useful item instead of the specifically required one.
Selecting a line with a familiar name without checking its exact function.
Using a lifeboat procedure to answer a liferaft question.
